text: The surgeon begins the perineal phase by incising the intersphincteric sulcus. A lone star retractor is used to aid in the intersphincteric dissection of the anal canal. The anus is closed with a Hansen purse string suture to maintain pneumo-rectum. The anal-vaginal fistula is isolated and detached, allowing dissection up to the pelvic floor. Pneumorectum is induced using a single port device with the GelPOINT path.

With pneumorectum maintained at fifteen millimeters of mercury, the surgeon begins a closed rectal dissection using standard laparoscopic instruments, carefully avoiding injury to nerves and adjacent structures until reaching the peritoneal reflection.
During the perineal dissection, the surgeon simultaneously closes the loop ileostomy in the abdomen, leaving it intraperitoneally. The excess tissue is utilized for the single-port gel point access.
The surgeon establishes a pneumoperitoneum in the abdominal cavity at fifteen millimeters of mercury pressure, similar to the pressure used during the rectal phase of the procedure.
In the abdominal cavity, the surgeon performs adhesiolysis to mobilize the iliorectal anastomosis. The ileum is resected proximal to the anastomosis using an endoscopic stapler.
The surgeon performs a transperineal extraction of the specimen, ensuring sufficient length of the ileum loop is available for the placement of a new terminal ileostomy.
